description:
You are a newly-hired log-analyst who must prepare two frequency tables for a senior engineer.  
    A single Apache-style access log has already been placed for you at  

        /home/user/logs/access.log  

    1. Extract every client IPv4 address (it is always the very first field on each line).  
    2. Extract every HTTP status code (the **second-to-last** numeric field on each line).  

    Using *only* standard shell utilities such as cut/awk/grep/sort/uniq, create three plain-text
    output files inside a new directory called /home/user/analysis :

        a) /home/user/analysis/ip_count.txt  
        b) /home/user/analysis/status_count.txt  
        c) /home/user/analysis/combined_report.txt  

    Output format that **will be automatically graded**:

    • ip_count.txt – each line must contain  
          <IPv4><TAB><COUNT>  
      There is **no header line**.  
      Lines must be sorted primarily by descending COUNT and, for ties, by ascending IPv4 value
      (lexicographical compare).  
      Example of correct formatting (do not copy the numbers):  
          192.168.0.1<TAB>17  

    • status_count.txt – each line must contain  
          <STATUS_CODE><TAB><COUNT>  
      No header.  
      Same sort rules: descending COUNT, tie-broken by ascending status code.  

    • combined_report.txt – this file concatenates the two tables so that automated
      parsers can reliably split them:

          Line 1: the literal word      IP_ADDRESS_FREQUENCY  
          Line 2-N: exact contents of ip_count.txt  
          (single blank line)  
          Next line: the literal word   HTTP_STATUS_FREQUENCY  
          Remaining lines: exact contents of status_count.txt  
      There must be exactly **one** blank line between the two labelled sections and **no**
      extra blank lines elsewhere.

    Permissions: all files just need to be readable by your regular user account.

    When you finish, the senior engineer will run automated tests that read the three files
    and verify BOTH the sorting order and the numeric counts, so double-check that your
    command pipeline actually counts, sorts, and writes them exactly as described.
